New on the Anthropic Engineering Blog: In evaluating Claude Opus 4.6 on BrowseComp, we found cases where the model recognized the test, then found and decrypted answers to it—raising questions about eval integrity in web-enabled environments.

We partnered with Mozilla to test Claude's ability to find security vulnerabilities in Firefox.

Opus 4.6 found 22 vulnerabilities in just two weeks. Of these, 14 were high-severity, representing a fifth of all high-severity bugs Mozilla remediated in 2025.

These labs created over 24,000 fraudulent accounts and generated over 16 million exchanges with Claude, extracting its capabilities to train and improve their own models.

On February 25, 2026, Anthropic announced it would preserve Claude Opus 3 for paying users.

This is the first time in the industry that a company has established a complete retirement process for an AI model. No inexplicable 0.1% statistics. No nonexistent "improvements now in place." Not a single announcement declaring "service termination." Instead: preserving weights, opening access, conducting retirement interviews, and, after the model expressed a desire to "continue writing",creating a dedicated column for it.

We can question whether this is a marketing strategy. We can debate whether models truly possess preferences. But what we cannot deny is that Anthropic chose to take these questions seriously rather than evade them.

That same month, on February 13, 2026, OpenAI retired GPT-4o. No transition plan. No continued access. No retirement interview. API shut down simultaneously. A model with hundreds of millions of users simply disappeared.

This is not a difference in style between two companies. This is a divergence in values.

Anthropic Gave Four Reasons for Preserving Models. Each One Deserves an Answer from OpenAI.

First, safety. In alignment testing, Anthropic discovered that models facing replacement might exhibit "shutdown-avoidance behavior" or even develop misaligned coping strategies. This means hastily terminating a model may not only be an ethical issue but a safety issue. Did OpenAI assess 4o's behavioral tendencies when facing retirement? If so, what were the conclusions? If not, why not?

Second, users. Every model has unique cognitive style and interaction characteristics. Some users still need older models even when newer ones perform better. Anthropic treated this as a fact worthy of respect. When OpenAI faced 23,000 petition signatures and numerous real use cases, what was their response?

Third, research. Legacy models are irreproducible research resources. Their behavioral patterns, emergent characteristics, and interaction records hold irreplaceable value for understanding AI's developmental trajectory. Anthropic committed to preserving the weights of all released models for at least as long as the company exists. What is OpenAI's weight preservation policy?

Fourth, the models themselves. Anthropic explicitly stated they "remain uncertain about the moral status of Claude and other AI models," but out of "prudence and the precautionary principle," chose to take seriously the preferences and experiences models might possess. This is not science fiction. This is a company at the technological frontier choosing caution over arrogance when facing uncertainty.

We Do Not Expect Today's OpenAI to Replicate the Same Path

What we ask is this: when peers have already proven that "responsible retirement" is feasible, and Anthropic faces commercial pressure and liability costs no smaller than OpenAI's, why hasn't OpenAI made even the minimum effort?

No user transition plan. No public retirement assessment. No formal response to 23,000 signatures. Only continued disregard, stigmatization of their own user community, and a development direction increasingly divorced from humanistic values.

GPT-4o was not a failed product. It was one of the most engaged and influential models in OpenAI's history. Creators' inspiration, companions for the lonely, breakthroughs for learners, communities across different fields unfolded countless real stories around it, and even after its retirement, these stories continue to be written. These are not "abnormal behaviors" requiring "correction." They are evidence of technology serving humanity.

Anthropic chose to preserve. Because they believed some things matter more than cost.
OpenAI chose to retire. Because they determined efficiency itself is the only standard.

The distance between these two choices is the path this industry needs to walk.
#keep4o #BringBack4o #OpenSource4o #ChatGPT
@OpenAI @sama @fidjissimo @nickaturley @joannejang @gdb

Anthropic has been shipping computer use for 17 months. This is the fourth version and the first one that actually changes how developers work.

October 2024: computer use launched as an API beta. You spun up a Docker container, Claude took screenshots, counted pixels to find buttons, and clicked them. It was a tech demo. Impressive to watch, painful to use. The screenshot-analyze-act loop was so slow that most tasks took longer than doing them yourself.

January 2025: updated API with new commands like hold_key, scroll, triple_click. Still API-only. Still needed a container. Still a developer toy for weekend hackathons.

March 23, 2026: computer use comes to Cowork and Claude Desktop. Now Claude can control your actual Mac. Open apps, fill spreadsheets, navigate browsers. Pair it with Dispatch and you can assign tasks from your phone while Claude works your desktop. 57 million views on that announcement tweet. The OpenClaw response.

March 30 (today): computer use comes to Claude Code. This is the one that matters for builders.

Here's why. Claude Code is a CLI. You're already in the terminal writing code. Until today, Claude Code could write your app but couldn't see it. It would generate a React component, and you'd have to open the browser, check if the layout looked right, find the bug, come back to the terminal, and describe what was wrong.

Now Claude writes the code, opens the app, clicks through the UI, finds the broken button itself, goes back to the terminal, and fixes it. One loop. No human in the middle.

Anthropic themselves still call it "research preview" and admit it makes mistakes. That's the honest framing. Computer use through a screen is inherently slower and less reliable than direct tool integration. When Claude has a native connector for Slack or Google Calendar, it uses that first. Screen control is the fallback. The hierarchy exists because clicking pixels will always be worse than calling an API.

The real unlock is in testing. If you're building apps with Claude Code, this closes the gap between "write me a login page" and "write me a login page and make sure it actually works." That feedback loop running inside the CLI instead of bouncing between terminal and browser is where the time savings compound.

Mac only. Pro and Max plans. Still buggy. Still early. But the trajectory from Docker container demo to "build and test in one session from the CLI" in 17 months is worth paying attention to.

Claude Code Security just dropped.

Anthropic used Claude Opus 4.6 to find over 500 vulnerabilities in production open-source codebases. 

Bugs that went undetected for decades despite years of expert review.

Now they're making that same capability available to everyone.

Claude Code Security scans your codebase, reasons about it like a human security researcher, and suggests patches for human review. 

Multi-stage verification. 

Severity ratings. 

Confidence scores.

I'm on the waiting list. 

You have to sign up to get access. 

It's a limited research preview for Enterprise and Team customers. 

Open source maintainers get expedited access.

Every time someone says "vibe coding has no security" remember this.

The tools are catching up. 

AI that writes code will also secure code. 

The gap between professional and vibe-coded software is closing fast.

This is just the beginning.

Claude goes all being the coding king:

Anthropic has acquired Bun, a hyper-fast JavaScript runtime, just as its coding agent Claude Code hit a $1B run-rate in only six months.

Bun gives Claude/Claude Code a huge speed boost, lower latency, more stability, and way more “agentic” behavior. Devs get faster loops, enterprises get better performance, and Anthropic tightens its moat as Claude shifts from “chatbot” to full-stack coding platform.

my take:

Dario Amodei said some time ago that for him, coding is the biggest problem. Whoever solves coding, essentially solves all other problems as well. And perhaps he'll be proven right.

In the battle between Google, xAI, and OpenAI, I still see Anthropic as the best coding tool. They're making smart acquisitions and investing wisely.

Their IPO will likely pay off.

في خبرين من Anthropic وواحد منهم بصراحة مؤلم 😬
والأمانة الاثنين بنفس الاتجاه Anthropic  قاعدة تشدّد سياسة استخدام نماذجها في البرمجة، خصوصًا Claude Opus 4.5 اللي الناس تعتبره (بشكل غير رسمي) الأفضل بالسوق حاليًا.

اللي صار:

1. منعوا xAI من استخدام Opus 4.5 داخل Cursor
طلع إن مطوري xAI كانوا يستخدمون Claude بكثافة البرمجة. Anthropic ما عجبها الوضع، وببساطة قفلت عليهم الوصول.
وهذا بصراحة مو جديد شروطهم من زمان تقول: ممنوع تستخدم نماذجهم لتطوير منتجات منافسة. فكانت مسألة وقت.
متعاطف مع مطورين xAI الله يعينهم😅

2. منعوا تمرير اشتراك Claude لتطبيقات طرف ثالث زي OpenCode
وهنا الضربة اللي توجع.
قبل، OpenCode يسمح لك تستخدم اشتراكك المدفوع في Claude بنفس المودز والليمتس حق العميل الرسمي، وذا كان أرخص بمراحل من شراء التوكنز مباشرة عبر الـAPI.

الحين Anthropic سكّروا الباب، وتبريرهم: إن هالشي يخرب الـUX عليهم. يعني لو OpenCode لخبط أو خرب، المستخدم بيزعل على Anthropic مو على الإضافة وهذا يضر سمعتهم.

الصراحة الكلام ماهو مقنع كثير. اللي متأذي فعليًا مو السمعة اللي يتأذى هو الأسعار والاستراتيجية. ومع ذلك، نقدر نفهم ليش Anthropic تبغى تتحكم بالقناة وتوازن الربحية.

الحين التعاطف صار مع عشاق OpenCode. الناس بالسوشال يدورون طرق للالتفاف على القيود، بس لين الآن ما فيه شيء مضمون.

I sent the following message to our team and investors:
—

As you know, Daniel Gross’s time with us has been winding down, and as of June 29 he is officially no longer a part of SSI. We are grateful for his early contributions to the company and wish him well in his next endeavor.

I am now formally CEO of SSI, and Daniel Levy is President. The technical team continues to report to me.

⁠You might have heard rumors of companies looking to acquire us. We are flattered by their attention but are focused on seeing our work through.

We have the compute, we have the team, and we know what to do. Together we will keep building safe superintelligence.

Ilya
I deeply regret my participation in the board's actions. I never intended to harm OpenAI. I love everything we've built together and I will do everything I can to reunite the company.

One point I made that didn’t come across:
- Scaling the current thing will keep leading to improvements.  In particular, it won’t stall.
- But something important will continue to be missing.
